SUMMARY:
By applying blockchain in supply chain management, businesses enable secure audit trails, ensuring trustworthy logistics synchronization, real-time shipment monitoring, and automated settlements. This is where blockchain and supply chain management meet to ensure transparency — the key success metric. With blockchain in SCM, transactions are time-stamped and up-to-date, sending the data to logistics management systems and supply chain analytics software in real time. Using blockchain in supply chains ensures enhanced transparency, improved traceability, increased efficiency, reduced fraud, lower costs, and better security through immutable and decentralized data records. ### How does blockchain enhance transparency in supply chain management? ### How does blockchain enhance security in supply chain management? Blockchain enhances security in supply chain management by using cryptographic techniques to create immutable and tamper-proof records, ensuring data integrity.

FULL CONTENT:
![logo](/img/base/logo.svg)

# Why and How to Employ Blockchain in Supply Chain Management (Tips & Success Stories)

![facebook](/img/svg/socials/facebook.svg)
![linkedin](/img/svg/socials/linkedin.svg)
![twitter](/img/svg/socials/tweeter.svg)
![telegram](/img/svg/socials/telegram.svg)
![blockchain and supply chain management best implementation practices](/img/articles/why-and-how-to-employ-blockchain-in-supply-chain-management-tips-and-success-stories/img01.jpg "By implementing blockchain for supply chain management, businesses can ensure secure and automated transactions, potentially saving operational")

**Key Takeaways**

Blockchain supply chain software enhances transparency and reduces costs by eliminating intermediaries.

In 2024, 47% of Inspectorio survey respondents identified blockchain as a crucial tech advancement to embrace for visibility and automation.

By applying blockchain in supply chain management, businesses enable secure audit trails, ensuring trustworthy logistics synchronization, real-time shipment monitoring, and automated settlements.

The blockchain supply chain market, valued at $3.25B in 2025, is expected to grow at a 59.8% CAGR, reaching $21.29B by 2029.

Since logistics has become a highly automated and digitized sphere, each of the many moving parts requires comprehensive oversight. As goods move from one location to another, it can be challenging to track all the transactions accurately within outdated [supply chain systems](/blog/supply-chain-management-software-system-its-features-benefits-and-how-to-integrate-a-suitable-one/).

This is where blockchain and supply chain management meet to ensure transparency — the key success metric. Considered one of the most impactful [logistics tech trends](/blog/top-logistics-technology-trends/), blockchain technology doesn’t only validate and store digital transactions; it protects systems from fraudulent activities. In fact, [47%](https://2325471.fs1.hubspotusercontent-na1.net/hubfs/2325471/State%20of%20Supply%20Chain%202024/20240522-PL-RP-StateofSupplyChain2024.pdf) of companies view blockchain as vital for visibility.

With 10+ years of automating and strengthening logistics operations with AI/[ML](/blog/adopting-machine-learning-in-supply-chain-and-logistics-for-successful-automation/), [IoT](/blog/embracing-iot-in-transportation-and-logistics-to-boost-supply-chain-visibility/), and blockchain-based tools, Acropolium has gained strategic insights for advanced tech implementations.

Today, we will explore how blockchain in SCM can enhance transparency, traceability, and efficiency with use cases, success cases, and best practices.

## What is Blockchain in Supply Chain Operations?

![statistics and projections of blockchain in supply chain management](/img/articles/why-and-how-to-employ-blockchain-in-supply-chain-management-tips-and-success-stories/img02.jpg "The blockchain supply chain market is seeing rapid growth and is estimated to reach $21.29 Billion in 2029.")

![statistics and projections of blockchain in supply chain management](/img/articles/why-and-how-to-employ-blockchain-in-supply-chain-management-tips-and-success-stories/img02.jpg "The blockchain supply chain market is seeing rapid growth and is estimated to reach $21.29 Billion in 2029.")

Blockchain is a decentralized ledger for storing and recording transactions securely, initially introduced for Bitcoin in 2008. It links transaction blocks in a chronological, immutable chain, verified by multiple participants without central control.

The combined power of blockchain and supply chain management lies in the secure operations of all logistics parties on a shared, usually [cloud-based](/blog/cloud-computing-in-logistics-and-supply-chain/) platform. This technology ensures a trusted, tamper-resistant audit trail, allowing for synchronized logistics, shipment monitoring, and automated settlements with minimal system changes.

Thus, anyone from manufacturers and shippers to carriers and brokers can maintain comprehensive records and prevent data breaches. And, with the huge impact on core industry activities, the blockchain supply chain market is [forecasted](https://www.thebusinessresearchcompany.com/report/blockchain-supply-chain-global-market-report) to grow impressively. Valued at $3.27B in 2025, it is anticipated to expand at a CAGR of 59.8% and reach $21.29B by 2029.

## Why Operate with Blockchain in the Supply Chain?

![benefits of blockchain in SCM](/img/articles/why-and-how-to-employ-blockchain-in-supply-chain-management-tips-and-success-stories/img03.jpg "When supply chain management and blockchain technology meet in logistics operations, they help businesses enhance transparency, improve traceability, and strenghten transactional security.")

![benefits of blockchain in SCM](/img/articles/why-and-how-to-employ-blockchain-in-supply-chain-management-tips-and-success-stories/img03.jpg "When supply chain management and blockchain technology meet in logistics operations, they help businesses enhance transparency, improve traceability, and strenghten transactional security.")

In 2024, [43.5%](https://content.beacon.com/hubfs/Guides/Supply%20Chain%20Visibility%20Capabilities%20Report%202024.pdf) of companies faced challenges in sharing information with logistics partners, emphasizing the need for better supply chain visibility. Indeed, the transparency brought by blockchain supply chain software can cut costs by eliminating intermediaries. At the same time, it reduces administrative overheads from development to delivery and returns.

Cost savings, however, are not the only ways you can benefit from blockchain technology.

### Enhanced Transparency

By integrating blockchain through smart sensors and RFID tags, companies can accurately track goods through the supply chain. Such IoT and blockchain-powered tools record conditions such as temperature and humidity at each stage.

With blockchain in SCM, transactions are time-stamped and up-to-date, sending the data to [logistics management systems](/blog/why-a-logistics-management-system-is-a-must-for-successful-supply-chain-operations/) and [supply chain analytics software](/blog/employing-supply-chain-analytics-software-for-efficient-workflows-key-features-use-cases/) in real time.

### Improved Traceability

In supply chain management, traceability refers to tracking inventory locations and custody from raw materials to end customers. Blockchain enables efficient live tracking with decentralized ledgers. Often found in [multi-carrier software](/blog/multi-carrier-shipping-software-a-guide-to-enhancing-shipping-efficiency/), the technology allows stakeholders to access detailed product information such as dates, prices, origin, quality, and certification.

### Increased Efficiency

Blockchain technology in supply chain management also contributes to timely resolved compliance problems and automated workflows, resulting in higher efficiency. The enhanced visibility helps detect and address issues like compliance violations, counterfeits, delays, and waste more rapidly.

In fact, the [Beacon’s report](https://content.beacon.com/hubfs/Guides/Supply%20Chain%20Visibility%20Capabilities%20Report%202024.pdf) says that blockchain is set out to be a game-changer in adherence to regulatory compliance within supply chains.

### Stronger Security

The cryptographic features protect sensitive information, making it harder to access or alter data for unauthorized entities. Supply chain and blockchain transparency allows all stakeholders to verify transactions independently, lowering the risk of compromise and errors.

Smart contracts automate processes, enable immediate invoice payments, and establish a transparent payment system, thereby reducing human error and fraud.

## Use Cases and Applications of Blockchain in Supply Chains

![blockchain supply chain use cases and applications](/img/articles/why-and-how-to-employ-blockchain-in-supply-chain-management-tips-and-success-stories/img04.jpg "Discover the globally adopted blockchain supply chain management use cases to learn how your business can benefit from the secure technology.")

![blockchain supply chain use cases and applications](/img/articles/why-and-how-to-employ-blockchain-in-supply-chain-management-tips-and-success-stories/img04.jpg "Discover the globally adopted blockchain supply chain management use cases to learn how your business can benefit from the secure technology.")

There are many companies using blockchain in supply chain operations, and each of them fulfills different needs with the technology. Now, let’s explore the most common blockchain supply chain use cases that represent the above business benefits.

### Provenance Tracking

Blockchain enables accurate tracking of a product’s origin, ensuring authenticity. However, ensuring data accuracy remains critical, as blockchain systems are only as reliable as the data input into them. For example, De Beers uses blockchain to track diamonds from mines to retailers, thus preventing conflict diamonds from entering the market.

In logistics, companies like Maersk use blockchain to monitor the transportation of goods from origin to destination, enhancing traceability. As for manufacturing, companies such as Siemens apply blockchain to track the production process of high-value parts. This way, businesses ensure quality and authenticity throughout the supply chain.

### Counterfeit Prevention

Blockchain in SCM helps verify product authenticity, reducing counterfeit goods. IBM and Walmart, using progressive [logistics and shipping APIs](/blog/a-guide-to-integrating-logistics-and-shipping-apis-to-optimize-your-supply-chain-business/), have implemented blockchain to trace the origin of food products. This way, the companies ensure food safety and prevent contamination.

Retail luxury brands like Louis Vuitton and Prada use blockchain to authenticate high-end goods, guaranteeing that customers receive genuine products. Data accuracy issues are vital here too, as incorrect information on a product’s origin or certification could lead to trust issues with consumers.

In the pharmaceutical industry, leaders like Pfizer leverage blockchain to verify the authenticity of drugs, preventing counterfeit medicines from reaching consumers.

### Inventory Management

Blockchain provides real-time inventory tracking, reducing discrepancies. Maersk and IBM’s TradeLens platform uses blockchain to track shipping containers and [warehousing automation](/blog/chatbots-for-hospitality-and-travel-industries/), improving visibility and reducing delays.

Scalability becomes an issue here as managing the real-time data flow from thousands of containers across a vast network requires a highly scalable solution to avoid bottlenecks.

Getting back to retail, Walmart utilizes blockchain to improve stock management across its stores, ensuring timely restocking and minimizing out-of-stock situations. Manufacturers like Toyota also use blockchain to monitor parts inventory, ensuring parts are available when needed and preventing production delays.

### Automated Payments

Smart contracts on blockchain automate payments upon delivery, reducing administrative overhead. BHP Billiton uses blockchain to streamline supplier payments, ensuring timely and accurate transactions.

FedEx, a logistics company, automates payment processes between suppliers, carriers, and customers for seamless financial exchanges. As a retail giant, Amazon is also exploring blockchain in SCM to automate vendor payments, improving cash flow management and eliminating transactional delays.

### Tokenization of Assets

Tokenization allows the conversion of physical and intangible assets into digital tokens, which can be easily traded and tracked. Real estate companies like Propy use blockchain to tokenize property, enabling buyers and sellers to securely transfer ownership without intermediaries.

Financial companies like Securitize tokenize securities, allowing for fractional ownership and broader market access. In manufacturing, asset-heavy industries such as aerospace can tokenize equipment and machinery, allowing for easier transfer of ownership and streamlined maintenance tracking.

### Smart Contracts in Supply Chain

With smart contracts, you can automate and enforce agreements in the supply chain, ensuring supply chain and blockchain transparency for all parties. As an example, DHL uses blockchain-based contracts to promptly release payments when shipments meet specific delivery milestones.

But, the challenge lies in ensuring that all participants input accurate data, as discrepancies could delay or invalidate contract execution. Thus, in the automotive industry, BMW uses blockchain-based smart contracts to verify the completion of supplier obligations.

### Emphasizing Transparency & Trust

As blockchain builds trust with traceable records of transactions, companies like Nestlé use it to allow consumers to trace the journey of their food products. As a result, you can foster confidence in product quality and sourcing practices.

In logistics, USPS implements blockchain to offer clients full visibility into shipping statuses and transport conditions, strengthening the reputation of their services.

## Challenges and Considerations in Implementing Blockchain in the Supply Chain

![blockchain supply chain challenges and solutions](/img/articles/why-and-how-to-employ-blockchain-in-supply-chain-management-tips-and-success-stories/img05.jpg "When introducing blockchain in the supply chain operations, businesses can face certain challenges, which one can overcome with an agile approach.")

![blockchain supply chain challenges and solutions](/img/articles/why-and-how-to-employ-blockchain-in-supply-chain-management-tips-and-success-stories/img05.jpg "When introducing blockchain in the supply chain operations, businesses can face certain challenges, which one can overcome with an agile approach.")

### Complexity and Fragmentation

Embracing blockchain in supply chain management can be complex due to the fragmented nature of global supply chains, which involve multiple stakeholders, systems, and processes. Integrating blockchain technology across diverse systems and ensuring compatibility can be challenging.

Companies can start with pilot projects before scaling up, establish industry standards and interoperability with [big data](/blog/chatbots-for-hospitality-and-travel-industries/) frameworks, and collaborate with stakeholders to align on common goals and technical requirements to overcome complexity and fragmentation.

### Lack of Transparency

Despite blockchain’s disruptive potential, achieving transparency is challenging due to stakeholder resistance and reluctance to share data openly.

For such issues, you have to build trust by demonstrating transparency benefits like increased efficiency, reduced fraud, and improved traceability. Try adopting incentive mechanisms for data sharing and use permissioned blockchains to allow controlled access to sensitive information, addressing privacy concerns while enhancing transparency.

### Inefficiencies and Delays

Traditional supply chains often face inefficiencies and delays due to manual processes, redundant paperwork, and lack of real-time information. Moreover, employing blockchain for supply chain management requires re-engineering these processes, which can disrupt operations and face resistance.

To deal with it, businesses should conduct thorough process analysis and redesign workflows to leverage blockchain’s strengths, such as [shipping automation](/blog/how-to-automate-the-shipping-process-with-shipping-management-system/) and real-time data sharing. Integrating blockchain with IoT devices and smart contracts can automate data collection and processing, reducing downtimes and improving overall productivity.

### Security Concerns

Although blockchain technology is secure, vulnerabilities like coding errors, poor key management, and insecure interfaces can arise during implementation.

Vendors providing blockchain supply chain solutions follow best practices such as rigorous testing, regular audits, encryption, and secure coding standards. It’s also crucial to operate with multi-layered security measures like multi-signature authentication and secure APIs. Lastly, continuous monitoring and updating can help identify and mitigate security threats promptly.

### Integration with Emerging Technologies

Integrating blockchain can be complex and costly, often requiring extensive upgrades and careful planning to prevent disruptions. It can be an especially troublesome endeavor if you’re operating with legacy software or outdated tools, and the same goes for IoT or AI modules.

Compatibility issues may demand custom solutions, increasing both time and expenses. That is, you need to look for custom solutions, where functionality is focused on dealing with inefficiencies during the transition.

## Final Thoughts

A decentralized, immutable record of transactions allows stakeholders to track goods from source to delivery, enhancing transparency. However, widespread adoption is limited due to the required expertise and varying governmental regulations.

As blockchain technology matures, it will gradually replace traditional supply chain processes, though the transition will be incremental. And Acropolium is here to guide you in adopting this rising trend.

## FAQ

### What are the main benefits of using blockchain in supply chains?

Using blockchain in supply chains ensures enhanced transparency, improved traceability, increased efficiency, reduced fraud, lower costs, and better security through immutable and decentralized data records.

### How does blockchain enhance transparency in supply chain management?

Blockchain promotes accuracy and transparency in supply chain management by providing an immutable and decentralized ledger. It records all transactions and keeps them visible to authorized participants. This ensures that every step of the process is traceable and verifiable.

### How does blockchain enhance security in supply chain management?

Blockchain enhances security in supply chain management by using cryptographic techniques to create immutable and tamper-proof records, ensuring data integrity. Decentralization is designed to minimize the risk of a single point of failure, while smart contracts enforce secure transactions.

### How does blockchain handle data privacy concerns in supply chains?

Blockchain handles data privacy concerns in supply chains by using encryption to protect data, allowing only authorized access. It employs permissioned blockchains to restrict data visibility and ensures that sensitive information is shared securely through smart contracts.

### Is blockchain technology scalable for large supply chains?

Blockchain technology faces scalability challenges for large supply chains. Still, solutions like layer-2 protocols, sharding, and improved consensus algorithms are being developed to enhance capacity and efficiency, making them more suitable for extensive supply chain networks.

# How Blockchain Is Used To Drive Supply Chain Transparency

With so many processes and businesses involved in just one supply chain, maintaining transparency can sometimes become a challenge. But, what exactly is supply chain transparency and why is it important? Supply chain transparency refers to the accessibility of information regarding the processes within a supply chain by internal and external stakeholders such as customers, investors, and regulators.

While supply chain transparency is important for several reasons, the most important one is the growing demand from consumers for more information about the origin, production, and ethical practices related to the products they purchase. Environmental impact and labor practices are examples. Customer is king goes the popular saying. Your organization must have transparent supply chains to remain competitive and profitable. Moreover, the call for supply chain transparency acts such as the [Business Supply Chain Transparency on Trafficking and Slavery Act of 2020](https://www.congress.gov/bill/116th-congress/house-bill/6279/text?r=6&s=1.) is indicative of the need for transparency at all levels of supply chains. Blockchain is one way you can enjoy the benefits of supply chain transparency by increasing consumer trust.

But how? This article will provide a comprehensive guide on how you can leverage the power of blockchain to achieve transparency and minimize supply chain disruptions.

## The Emergence of Blockchain in Supply Chain

The first thing that probably comes to mind when you hear the term blockchain solutions is Bitcoin. Discussion of blockchain for transparency in the supply chain was unlikely, at least until it became a buzzword in the industry. In recent years, blockchain’s uses have extended from cryptocurrency to other industries and sectors, such as supply chain.

The emergence of blockchain in supply chains began around 2014, with industry leaders referring to it as a technology that would enhance transparency in supply chains. And with that, many organizations, small and large began adopting supply chain transparency software.

For example, the [IBM Food Trust platform](https://www.ibm.com/products/supply-chain-intelligence-suite/food-trust), launched in 2016, focused on using blockchain to enhance traceability and transparency in the food supply chain. It enables stakeholders, including producers, distributors, retailers, and consumers, to access a secure and immutable record of the journey of food products, from farm to table. This not only helps in preventing foodborne illnesses by quickly identifying and addressing contamination issues but also builds consumer trust by providing detailed information about the origin and handling of food items.

## Mechanisms of Blockchain for Transparency

So, why blockchain for transparency in supply chain and not any other technology? Well, it all goes back to its features.

## Real-World Applications of Blockchain in Supply Chains

Since the adoption of blockchain for supply chain transparency, there are several real-world applications we can learn from, including:

### Streamlined Logistics

Blockchain streamlines logistics in two ways: traceability and transparency. The ability to trace the journey of products from the manufacturer to the end consumer is crucial in logistics. Blockchain’s immutable ledger enables detailed and transparent tracking of products at each stage of the supply chain, facilitating quick identification of issues and reducing the time needed for problem resolution. As for transparency, blockchain provides a shared, decentralized ledger that allows all involved parties to have real-time visibility into the entire supply chain. [Supply chain visibility](https://surgere.com/blog/7-benefits-of-supply-chain-visibility/) and transparency help in better decision-making which allows companies to proactively address issues such as delays, diversions, or damages.

One example of blockchain transparency in streamlining logistics is [TradeLens](https://www.ibm.com/mysupport/s/topic/0TO50000000IQPpGAO/tradelens?language=en_US), a blockchain-based platform for global trade created for Maersk by IBM. Although discontinued in 2013, TradeLens provided end-to-end visibility into the supply chain for Maersk and its partners, including shippers, port operators, and customs authorities, and in turn, accelerated the flow of goods.

### Counterfeit Prevention

The global market faces significant challenges related to counterfeit products, especially in industries, such as pharmaceuticals, electronics, and luxury goods. Blockchain offers a solution by providing a tamper-proof and transparent record of a product’s journey through the supply chain.

Blockchain’s immutability feature ensures that once manufacturers record information about a product in the blockchain, no one can change it. This ensures that the product’s origin and authenticity remain intact throughout the entire supply chain. Moreover, features like QR codes or RFID tags linked to the blockchain are essential for secure authentication. Consumers can scan these codes to verify the product quality and authenticity and access information about its manufacturing, shipment, and distribution.

### Smart Contracts

Smart contracts are self-executing contracts with the terms directly written into code. In the context of supply chains, smart contracts on the blockchain automate and enforce contractual agreements, thereby reducing the need for intermediaries, minimizing the risk of disputes, and enhancing transparency. For example, upon successful delivery of goods, a smart contract can trigger the release of payment to the supplier, streamlining payment processes and reducing delays.

Additionally, the blockchain’s immutable feature ensures that once you deploy a smart contract on the blockchain, no one can change its terms without consensus from the network. This transparent execution of smart contracts enhances trust among participants.

## Future-Proof Your Supply Chain Transparency

Supply chain transparency is becoming increasingly essential, not just for stakeholders, but for the overall operational efficiency of supply chains. As such, to avoid common supply chain risks related to the lack of transparency and future-proofing your supply chain, you can leverage [Surgere’s SaaS solutions](https://surgere.com/solutions/), which seamlessly integrate IoT technology and a diverse array of tagging and hardware options.

With Surgere, you get real-time insights into your supply chains, safeguarding you against potential risks associated with insufficient transparency at any level of the supply chain. [Contact us today](https://surgere.com/contact-surgere/), and let us help you attain supply chain transparency.

--- SOURCE 3: What are the Benefits of Blockchain? ---
URL: https://www.ibm.com/think/topics/benefits-of-blockchain

SUMMARY:
# Benefits of blockchain ## What are the benefits of blockchain? The benefits of blockchain are increasing trust, security and transparency among member organizations by improving the traceability of data shared across a business network, plus delivering cost savings through new efficiencies. Beyond matters of trust, blockchain delivers even more business benefits, including the cost savings from increased speed, efficiency and automation. ## How industries benefit from blockchain Building trust between trading partners, providing end-to-end visibility, streamlining processes and resolving issues faster with blockchain all add up to stronger, more resilient supply chains and better business relationships. In an industry troubled by data breaches, blockchain can help healthcare improve security for patient data while making it easier to share records across providers, payers and researchers.

FULL CONTENT:
# Benefits of blockchain

## What are the benefits of blockchain?

The benefits of blockchain are increasing trust, security and transparency among member organizations by improving the traceability of data shared across a business network, plus delivering cost savings through new efficiencies.

### Building trust and boosting the bottom line

Blockchain for business uses a shared and immutable ledger that only members with permission can access. Network members control what information each organization or member can see, and what actions each can take. Blockchain is sometimes called a “trustless” network, not because business partners don’t trust each other, but because *they don’t have to*.

This trust is built on blockchain’s enhanced security, greater transparency and instant traceability. Beyond matters of trust, blockchain delivers even more business benefits, including the cost savings from increased speed, efficiency and automation. By greatly reducing paperwork and errors, blockchain significantly reduces overhead and transaction costs, and reduces or eliminates the need for third parties or middlemen to verify transactions.

### IBM Blockchain

Our clients have a vision for how blockchain will change their business, and we have an approach to make it happen. Let's co-create successful and growing business networks together.

## Five important blockchain benefits

Your data is sensitive and crucial, and blockchain can significantly change how you view your critical information. By creating a record that can’t be altered and is encrypted end-to-end, the blockchain helps prevent fraud and unauthorized activity.

You can address privacy issues on the blockchain by anonymizing personal data and by using permissions to prevent access. A network of computers, rather than a single server, stores information, making it difficult for hackers to view data.

Without blockchain, each organization has to keep a separate database. Because blockchain uses a distributed ledger, it records transactions and data identically in multiple locations.

All network participants with permissioned access see the same information at the same time, providing full transparency. All transactions are immutably recorded, and are time- and date-stamped. This capability enables members to view the entire history of a transaction and virtually eliminates any opportunity for fraud.

Blockchain creates an audit trail that documents the provenance of an asset at every step on its journey. In industries where consumers are concerned about environmental or human rights issues surrounding a product or an industry troubled by counterfeiting and fraud, this helps provide the proof.

With blockchain, it is possible to share data about provenance directly with customers. Traceability data can also expose weaknesses in any supply chain, where goods might sit on a loading dock awaiting transit.

Traditional paper-heavy processes are time-consuming, prone to human error, and often requires third-party mediation. By streamlining these processes with blockchain, you can complete transactions faster and more efficiently.

You can store documentation on the blockchain along with transaction details, eliminating the need to exchange paper. There’s no need to reconcile multiple ledgers, so clearing and settlement can be much faster.

“Smart contracts” can automate transactions, further increasing your efficiency and speeding up the process. After the pre-specified conditions are met, it automatically triggers the next step in the transaction or process.

Smart contracts reduce human intervention and reliance on third parties to verify the fulfillment of the contract terms. In insurance, for example, after a customer has provided all necessary documentation to file a claim, the system automatically settles and pays the claim.

![Overhead view of people working in a meeting room](/content/dam/worldwide-content/cdp/cf/ul/g/4b/9b/BTB_Card6RE_284x284.component.think-ad-xl.ts=1747665121347.png/content/adobe-cms/us/en/think/topics/benefits-of-blockchain/_jcr_content/root/table_of_contents/body-article-8/think_ad_copy_188453/image)

### Maximize hybrid cloud value in the generative AI era

Only 1 in 4 enterprises achieve a solid ROI from cloud transformation efforts. Learn how to amplify hybrid cloud and AI value across business needs.

## How industries benefit from blockchain

### Blockchain benefits in supply chain and food chain

Building trust between trading partners, providing end-to-end visibility, streamlining processes and resolving issues faster with blockchain all add up to stronger, more resilient supply chains and better business relationships. Plus, participants can act sooner in the event of disruptions. In the food industry, blockchain can help ensure food safety and freshness, and reduce waste. In the event of contamination, you can trace the food back to its source in seconds rather than days.

### Banking and financial industry blockchain benefits

When financial institutions replace old processes and paperwork with blockchain, they realize several benefits. These benefits include removing friction and delays, and increasing operational efficiencies across the industry, including global trade, trade finance, clearing and settlement, consumer banking, lending and other transactions.

### Healthcare blockchain benefits

In an industry troubled by data breaches, blockchain can help healthcare improve security for patient data while making it easier to share records across providers, payers and researchers. Control over access remains in the hands of the patient, increasing trust.

### Pharmaceutical blockchain benefits

As pharmaceutical products move through the supply chain, the system records every action. The resulting audit trail allows tracing an item from origin to pharmacy or retailer, helping to prevent counterfeiting and enabling manufacturers to locate a recalled product in seconds.

### Government blockchain benefits

Blockchain can help governments work smarter and innovate faster. Secure sharing of data between citizens and agencies can increase trust while providing an immutable audit trail for regulatory compliance, contract management, identity management and citizen services.

### Insurance blockchain benefits

Insurance companies are using blockchain and smart contracts to automate manual and paper-intensive processes such as underwriting and claims settlement, increasing speed and efficiency, and reducing costs. Blockchain’s faster, verifiable data exchanges help reduce fraud and abuse.

--- SOURCE 4: Using blockchain to drive supply chain transparency ---
URL: https://www.deloitte.com/us/en/services/consulting/articles/blockchain-supply-chain-innovation.html

SUMMARY:
For example, to increase transparency and traceability, companies in resource-intensive industries have turned to blockchain solutions to help control Scope 3 emissions.2 Finally, because global supply chains involve many discrete entities that are frequently separated by several degrees in terms of their interests, the quality and opacity of information invariably degrades trust among parties. For example, enterprise blockchain solutions can help streamline supply chains by enhancing security and trust, can facilitate the protection of personal data (such as medical records), and can help governments, among others, better protect and more effectively use citizen data. Given the current economic headwinds, and as corporations and consumers alike entertain high expectations of supply chains with fewer risks, greater visibility and transparency, and enhanced trust, Deloitte encourages enterprises to consider blockchain solutions as a possible antidote to their supply chain challenges.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Blockchain enhances traceability by providing an immutable record of transactions, allowing stakeholders to track product origins and movements, ensuring accountability throughout the supply chain.
By offering a decentralized ledger, blockchain promotes transparency, enabling real-time access to supply chain data, which builds consumer trust and meets regulatory compliance demands.

Blockchain automates processes through smart contracts, minimizing paperwork and errors, which leads to faster transactions, reduced administrative overhead, and significant cost savings in supply chain management.
